sutta: once when the exalted 1 was staying near zavati and jeter grove in anatapindi kaspar the venerable nandaka gave dhamma discourse to the monks in the service hall taught them roused them incited them and gladdened them let me stop here for a moment the verbal nandaka was supposed to be the chief among the monks who thought taught the other monks he was very good in teaching other monks and his arahant he was in arahan and the suta continues now in the evening the exalted 1 rose from seclusion and approached the service hall having come he stood outside the doorway waiting for the discourse to end and when he knew that the discourse was ended he coughed and tapped on the board then those monks opened the door to the exalted 1 and he entered the hall and sat down on the appointed seat when he had seated himself the exalted 1 spoke thus to the venerable nandaka surely nandaka this dhamma discourse which you preached the root to the monks was a long 1 my back ached as i stood outside the doorway waiting for the discourse to end when he had thus broken available nandaka being embarrassed said to him nay lord we knew not that the exalted 1 stood outside the door had we known lord we would not have said so much now the exalted 1 knew that the available nandaka was embarrassed so he said to him well done well done dhaka this is right for you clansmen who by faith have gone forth from the home to the homeless life when you may see when you may be seated around for dhamma discourse for you assemble nandaka there are 2 causes either discuss on dhamma or maintain the aryan silence i'll stop here from for a moment um so just just now we saw the buddha stood outside the door waiting for nandaka to finish speaking and this shows the buddha's humility now this continues among nandaka has faith but is not virtuous so in that part he is not perfect therefore that part must be perfected let him think good that i might have both faith and virtue and when nandaka the mom has both faith and virtue then is he in that part perfect among nandaka has faith and virtue but cannot obtain calm of mind within so in that part he is not perfect therefore that part must be perfected let him think good that i might have faith and virtue and obtain calm of mind within and when nandaka the monk has faith and virtue and can obtain calm of mind within then is he in that part perfect among nandaka has faith and virtue and can obtain calm of mind within but cannot obtain the higher wisdom by contemplation of dhamma so in that part he is not perfect just as some 4-footed creature may have 1 foot short and stunted and so not be perfect in that limb even so nandaka among has faith and virtue and can obtain calm of mind within but cannot obtain the higher wisdom by contemplation of dhamma so in that part he is not perfect therefore that part must be perfected let him think good that i might have faith and virtue and obtain calm of mind within and obtain the higher wisdom by contemplation of dhamma and when nandaka the monk has faith and virtue and can obtain calm of mind within and the higher wisdom by contemplation of dhamma then in that part he is perfect thus spoke the exalted 1 and when the exalted 1 had finished speaking he rose from his seat and entered his abode now not long after the exhorted 1 had gone the venable nandaka addressed the mount saying reverend sirs the exalted 1 has just set forth the holy life which is perfect and pure in its entirety in 4 steps and now he has got up and gone to his abode i'll just stop here to commend so here the buddha is teaching that the 4 steps in perfecting the holy life the first 1 faith refers to unshakable faith in the buddha dharma and sangha and when you obtain unshakable faith it means you have right view and in the majima nikaya number 43 there are 2 conditions to give us right view 1 is listening to the dhamma the other 1 is having proper attention so these 2 things if we listen to the dhamma that means they suit us and we pay attention then when you have uh listened to enough of the dhamma and of the sultasa then we have right view and when we have right view then we have unshakable faith the second 1 um virtue 1 that means the monk must keep the precepts cultivate the precepts and the third quality is calm of mind within them and this most probably refers to the jhanas because samadhi in the sutas always refer to the janus perfect samadhi is the 1 pointedness of mind and the fourth quality is contemplation the higher wisdom by contemplation of dhamma [Music] so we they obtain calm and then we practice contemplation of the dharma to obtain the wisdom so these are the 4 qualities and the suta continues and the venubal nandaka told the monks all that the exalted 1 had said continuing now there are references these 5 advantages from listening to dhamma at the proper time and from discussing dhamma at the proper time what 5 take the case references of a monk who teaches the monks dhamma lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle and lovely in the end setting forth both in spirit and in letter the holy life perfect and pure in its entirety references as often as the monk teaches the monks dhamma lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle and lovely in the end setting forth both in spirit and in letter the holy life perfect and pure in its entirety just so often is the teacher dear to him loved esteemed and reverenced by him this source is the first advantage from listening to dhamma at the proper time and from discussing dhamma at the proper time again as often as he teaches dhamma setting forth the holy life so often is he a partaker of the word and in the meaning of this dhamma this references is the second advantage again as often as he teaches dhamma so often does he see in that dhamma as he as he penetrates it by wisdom its profound import this references is the third advantage again as often as he teaches dhamma so often do his fellows in the holy life revere him the more saying of a truth this reverencer has attained or will attain this references is the fourth advantage then references in the case of a monk who teaches the monks dhamma lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle and lovely in the end setting forth both in spirit and in letter the holy life perfect and pure in its entirety as soon as the monk teaches dhamma and sets forth the holy life everywhere those monks who are but learners who have not attained mastery of the mind abide resolved on the unsurpassed peace from effort and having heard that dhamma strive with zeal to attain the unattained to master the unmastered to realize the unrealized and the arahan monks there who have destroyed the asavas lived the holy life done what was to be done lay down the burden attain perfection burst the bonds of becoming and are free through perfect knowledge on hearing dhamma to dwell comfortably here and now these references is the fifth advantage from listening to dhamma at the proper time and from discussing dhamma at the proper time reverend says these are the 5 advantages from listening to dhamma at the proper time and from discussing dhamma at the proper time is the end of the sutta
commentary: here the last part even though it says listening to dhamma and discussing dhamma actually these are the advantages of teaching dhamma the advantages of a monk teaching dhamma the first 1 as often as the monk teaches dhamma this so often is the buddha dear to him loved esteemed and revered by him this is the first advantage the second 1 as often as he teaches dhamma so often is he a partaker of the word and in the meaning of this dhamma number 3 as often as he teaches dhamma so often does he see in that dhamma is profound important or important he sees the importance of the dhamma he realizes the importance of the dhamma as he teaches number 4 as often as he teaches dharma so often do his fellows in the holy life revere him the more and number number 5 as he's as he teaches dhamma to the monks then the monks who have not attained then they strive to attain even harder we having heard the word of the buddha and even the arahants find comfort in the words of the buddha here and now so these are the advantages of teaching dharma next to times
